title: Rate Limiter — System Design
description: Designing a rate limiter that throttles API traffic per identity (user, IP, API key) according to configurable rules — starting from a single-process counter and evolving toward a distributed, edge-enforced limiter with a control plane.

functional: Reject requests that exceed a configurable threshold over a time window (e.g. 'user X: 100 req/min').
Support multiple scopes: per user, per IP, per API key, per endpoint, and combinations.
Return a clear rejection signal (HTTP 429) with Retry-After and remaining-quota headers.
Rules are configurable at runtime without redeploying the limiter.
Expose accurate metrics: allow/deny counts, top-limited principals, p99 decision latency.
nonFunctional: Very low decision latency (target ≤ 5 ms p99) — sits on every request.
High availability (≥ 99.99%) — outage should fail-open or degrade gracefully, not block production traffic.
Horizontally scalable to ≥ 1M decisions/sec across the fleet.
Accuracy: allowed traffic should stay within ~10% of the configured limit even under bursts.
Fault-tolerant: counter store outages must not bring down the protected service.
Multi-region: a single global limit and per-region limits both need to be expressible.

capacity.label: Decisions per second
capacity.note: One check per incoming request across the fleet

capacity.label: Decision latency budget
capacity.note: Sits in the request path; must be cheap

capacity.label: Metrics throughput
capacity.note: Async; one record per decision (sampled OK)

api.description: Synchronous check called by the gateway/middleware. Returns allow/deny + headers.
api.response: { allowed: bool, remaining: int, retryAfterMs: int, ruleId: "..." }
diagram: RL->>CS: INCR counter, get window state

api.description: Create or update a rate-limit rule (scope, threshold, window, action).
diagram: participant CP as Control Plane API
participant RL as Rate Limiter Fleet

api.description: List all rules, optionally filtered by scope/endpoint.
api.response: [ { id, scope, limit, windowMs, action, version, updatedAt } ]
diagram: participant CP as Control Plane API

api.description: Inspect remaining quota for a principal (debug / customer dashboard).
diagram: participant CP as Control Plane API

dataModel.name: rules
dataModel.note: Authoritative store of rate-limit rules. Read-heavy; cached aggressively in the rate-limiter process.

dataModel.name: rule_history
dataModel.note: Append-only audit log of rule changes. Operations only.

dataModel.name: counters (in-memory store)
dataModel.note: Lives in Redis or similar. Key = principal+rule; expires automatically when the window ends.

dataModel.name: decisions (analytics)
dataModel.note: Append-only stream of allow/deny outcomes. Written async via the metrics pipeline.

functional.requirement: Reject requests exceeding a configurable threshold over a time window.
functional.how: Each request is mapped to a (principal, rule) counter key. INCR returns the current count; if it exceeds the rule's limit_count, the limiter returns deny + Retry-After.

functional.requirement: Support per-user, per-IP, per-API-key, per-endpoint, and combined scopes.
functional.how: Rule scope is a first-class field. The principal extractor builds the counter key from the configured scope (e.g. 'user:42:posts.write', 'ip:1.2.3.4:global'), so the same machinery serves any scope.

functional.requirement: Clear rejection signal (HTTP 429) with Retry-After and remaining-quota headers.
functional.how: The gateway translates the limiter's deny response into 429 + Retry-After + X-RateLimit-Remaining headers based on the counter's TTL and the rule's limit_count.

functional.requirement: Runtime-configurable rules without redeploying the limiter.
functional.how: Rules live in a dedicated store with a versioned schema. The control plane writes new versions and publishes a change event; limiter processes pull and hot-swap their in-process cache.

functional.requirement: Expose accurate metrics for allow/deny, top-limited principals, decision latency.
functional.how: Every decision emits an async event to a metrics queue. A stream processor aggregates per-rule and per-principal counts; dashboards read from the analytics store.

nonFunctional.how: Decisions are a single INCR against an in-memory counter store. Rules are cached in-process; the gateway path adds at most a few µs around the network round trip.

nonFunctional.requirement: ≥ 99.99% availability — outage must not block production traffic.
nonFunctional.how: Counter-store failure trips a circuit breaker and the limiter fails open (allows traffic). Rule-store failure is masked by the in-process cache. Multi-AZ replicas for both stores.
Stateless limiter processes behind a load balancer. The counter store shards by key; the rule store is replicated read-only. The gateway-embedded fast path avoids cross-DC hops.

nonFunctional.requirement: Accuracy: allowed traffic within ~10% of the configured limit.
nonFunctional.how: Use a sliding-window algorithm (or token bucket) rather than naive fixed window — these bound the bursting that fixed-window suffers at boundaries.

nonFunctional.requirement: Counter-store outages must not take down the protected service.
nonFunctional.how: Circuit breaker around the counter calls. Local in-process counters serve as a degraded mode (less accurate but available). Operators can configure fail-open vs fail-closed per rule.

nonFunctional.requirement: Multi-region: global and per-region limits both expressible.
nonFunctional.how: Per-region limits use a local counter store. Global limits use a shared store with async replication (eventual; accept a few % over-allowance for the latency win). Rule schema includes a region scope.

followUps: How do you handle a thundering herd when a popular key's window resets?
How do you fail-open vs fail-closed safely, and on what signal?
How would you implement per-tenant tiered limits (free / pro / enterprise)?
How would you support burst budgets (e.g. token bucket with capacity 200, refill 10/s)?
How would the limiter behave during a counter-store failover?
How would you make the decision available at the CDN edge for cacheable requests?
How would you detect and respond to a real DDoS that overwhelms the limiter itself?

steps.title: 1. In-Process Counter (Single Server)
steps.description: Start with the simplest viable rate limiter: a single backend server keeps a counter per (principal, rule) in process memory. Each request increments the counter; if it exceeds the limit, the server returns 429.
steps.decisionPrompt: Start with the single-instance baseline: where is the counter checked, what correctness guarantee does it provide, and what traffic pattern breaks it?

concepts.definition: A counter stored in the memory of one running limiter process.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is fast and simple, but only correct while there is exactly one process.

concepts.definition: An HTTP response hint telling clients when to try again after being limited.
concepts.whyItMatters: Good rate limiters shape client behavior instead of only returning 429 errors.

deepDives.title: Counter representation
points: A map { key -> { count, windowStart } } updated on every request.
On window expiry, reset to zero. Simplest schedule: lazy reset checked at read time.
Memory cost is bounded by the number of active keys — a long-tail concern even on a single server.

bottlenecks.issue: All traffic must hit one server.
Counter lives in process — restart loses state and lets traffic burst.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Move counters to a durable, shared store.

talkingPoints: Acknowledge that this design only works at small scale; the value is establishing the data flow.
Mention that even at this stage you'd return Retry-After so clients can back off correctly.

flows.name: Single-server decision
flows.note: INCR happens entirely in process — no network call. Latency is whatever the request handler costs.

steps.title: 2. Distributed Backend, Shared Counter Problem
steps.description: Add a fleet of stateless backend servers behind a load balancer. The in-process counter approach now diverges: each server has its own count, so the effective limit is N × the configured limit. We need a shared, authoritative counter.
steps.decisionPrompt: Analyze the first scale-out failure: why do local counters diverge across servers, and what user-visible behavior results?

concepts.definition: A group of interchangeable servers that do not share local request state.
concepts.whyItMatters: It scales request handling, but local counters diverge across instances.

concepts.definition: A routing policy that sends the same client to the same server repeatedly.
concepts.whyItMatters: It can hide local-counter problems temporarily, but hurts balancing and failover.

deepDives.title: Why local counters break
points: Round-robin LB sprays a single user's requests across N servers; each server only sees 1/N of their traffic.
Sticky sessions (IP-hash) keep one user on one server, but trade load balance and complicate scale-in.
Even with stickiness, fleet restarts reshuffle keys and reset counters.

bottlenecks.issue: Local counters allow up to N× the configured limit through.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Centralize the counter in a shared store (next step).
Use centralized storage and round-robin LB.

talkingPoints: This step is mostly a motivation argument — the design hasn't changed except for the fleet, but the failure mode justifies what comes next.
Mention that gateways (envoy, nginx) face the same problem and adopt the same solution.

flows.name: Spray across the fleet (the broken case)
flows.note: Each server's local counter only sees a fraction of one user's traffic; the effective limit is N× higher than configured.
diagram: Note over S1,S2: Neither sees the global count

steps.title: 3. Centralized Counter Store
steps.description: Move counters to an in-memory shared store (Redis-style). Every limiter process executes an atomic INCR against a key derived from (principal, rule). The key has a TTL matching the window size so it auto-expires.
steps.decisionPrompt: Choose the shared counter store and request path: how does every limiter instance make an atomic decision without adding unacceptable latency?

concepts.definition: A read-modify-write operation that completes as one indivisible update.
concepts.whyItMatters: Concurrent limiter processes must not all observe the same old count and allow too much traffic.

concepts.definition: A shared low-latency store, often Redis-like, that owns rate-limit counters.
concepts.whyItMatters: It gives all limiter instances one authoritative decision point.

deepDives.title: Atomicity
points: A naïve GET+CHECK+SET races: two requests can both see count=99 and both write 100.
Use a single atomic operation: INCR then read result; reject if result > limit. Lua scripts can bundle INCR + EXPIRE atomically.
Token-bucket and sliding-window algorithms need slightly more state — a Lua script that does compare-and-update.

deepDives.title: Key TTL
points: Set EXPIRE on first INCR equal to the window size. The key auto-deletes when the window ends.
On window rollover, the next request creates a fresh key. No background cleanup needed.

deepDives.title: Fault tolerance
points: Counter-store outage is a critical risk — every API request now depends on it.
Wrap calls in a circuit breaker. On open, fall back to a local approximate counter or fail-open per rule policy.
Multi-AZ replicas; Cluster mode for sharding by key.

bottlenecks.issue: Counter store is on the critical path of every API request.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Circuit breaker + fail-open per rule; multi-AZ replicas; sharded cluster.

bottlenecks.issue: Hot keys (a single principal with huge traffic) overload one shard.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Client-side request coalescing; promote hot keys to dedicated shards; degrade to local approximation.

bottlenecks.issue: Fixed-window boundaries still allow bursts at edges.

talkingPoints: INCR + EXPIRE is the canonical pattern. Wrap it in a Lua script for atomicity.
Discuss the fail-open vs fail-closed decision — it depends on whether the service is more sensitive to outage or to abuse.

flows.name: Allowed request — shared INCR
flows.note: One atomic INCR per request; the response carries remaining quota for client headers.
diagram: S->>CS: INCR key (Lua: + EXPIRE if new)

flows.name: Rejected request — over limit
flows.note: If INCR returns a value above the rule's limit, deny and return Retry-After derived from the key's TTL.

flows.name: Counter-store outage — fail-open
flows.note: Circuit breaker trips after consecutive failures; the limiter allows traffic instead of taking down the API.
diagram: Note over S: alert: limiter degraded

steps.title: 3a. Choose the Right Algorithm
steps.description: A focused look at one aspect of the previous step. Fixed-window counters are simple but allow up to 2× the configured limit at window boundaries. Pick an algorithm that fits the traffic shape and accuracy needs. The architecture stays the same — only the Lua script in the counter store changes.
steps.decisionPrompt: Select the limiting algorithm for the product promise: compare fixed window, sliding window, and token bucket in terms of burst tolerance, memory, and fairness.

concepts.definition: Count requests inside discrete time buckets such as one minute.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is simple but can allow bursts at bucket boundaries.

concepts.definition: Refill tokens over time and spend one token per accepted request.
concepts.whyItMatters: It supports controlled bursts while preserving a long-term rate.

options.name: Fixed Window (baseline)
pros: O(1) memory per key, sub-ms decision
Easy to reason about and explain
cons: Boundary bursting: up to 2× the limit in a 2-window span
Doesn't reflect actual rate over a rolling interval
diagram: Algo[Fixed Window\n1 counter per key per window]

options.name: Sliding Window
pros: Affordable approximation (weighted previous + current window) is O(1)
cons: Exact sliding window (log of timestamps) is O(N) memory per key

options.name: Token Bucket
pros: Allows controlled bursts up to bucket capacity
Natural fit for 'X req/sec sustained, burst Y'
Widely understood; implemented by most gateways
cons: Two parameters (capacity + refill rate) — more to tune
Slightly trickier to expose remaining quota in a human-friendly way

deepDives.title: Other algorithms worth mentioning
points: Leaky bucket: smooths bursts by treating requests as drops; backpressure-style, less common for HTTP APIs.
Sliding-log: stores every request timestamp — most accurate, most expensive.
GCRA (generic cell rate algorithm): single timestamp per key, equivalent to leaky bucket with constant cost.

deepDives.title: Picking one
points: Default to sliding window approximation for most user-facing limits — best accuracy/cost trade.
Use token bucket when burst-tolerance is part of the contract (e.g. 'allow 200 burst, sustain 10/s').
Stay with fixed window only when accuracy is unimportant (debug, internal services).

bottlenecks.mitigation: Rule schema includes 'algorithm' field; the limiter dispatches to the right Lua script per rule.

bottlenecks.issue: Algorithm change is a behavior change.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Version the rule; dry-run new algo in shadow mode (decide but don't enforce) before flipping.

talkingPoints: Demonstrate awareness that this is a knob, not a one-time choice.
Mention shadow mode — running a new algorithm in parallel for comparison without affecting traffic.

flows.name: Sliding-window approximation
flows.note: Single Lua script reads previous + current window counts, weights the previous by the elapsed fraction, and compares to the limit.
diagram: S->>CS: EVAL slidingWindow(key, now, windowMs, limit)

flows.name: Token bucket
flows.note: On each request, refill tokens proportional to elapsed time, then try to consume one. State is { tokens, lastRefill }.
diagram: S->>CS: EVAL tokenBucket(key, now, capacity, refillPerMs)
CS->>CS: tokens = min(capacity, bucket.tokens + (now - bucket.lastRefill) * refillPerMs)

steps.title: 4. Rules Engine + Control Plane
steps.description: So far the limit is a constant in the limiter process. Operators need to add/change rules at runtime — per endpoint, per user tier, per region. Introduce a rule store and a control-plane API. Limiter processes cache rules in memory and refresh on change events.
steps.decisionPrompt: Design the control plane for live rule changes: how are rules versioned, distributed, cached, and rolled back without redeploying limiters?

concepts.definition: The administrative path used to configure and distribute rules.
concepts.whyItMatters: Operators need to change limits without redeploying every limiter.

concepts.definition: Attach a version to each rule so processes can tell which configuration they are using.
concepts.whyItMatters: Versioning makes rollout, audit, reconciliation, and rollback possible.

deepDives.title: Rule schema
points: rule_id, scope, limit_count, window_ms, action, algorithm, version, updated_at.
Scope can be composite: 'user AND endpoint' produces keys like 'user:42:endpoint:posts.write'.
Action: reject (429), shape (delay), log (count only). 'log' enables dry-run mode.

deepDives.title: Propagation
points: On write, the control plane bumps version and publishes a 'rules-changed' event.
Limiter processes subscribe and pull the changed rule, hot-swapping their in-process cache.
Periodic full reconciliation (every 60s) guards against missed events.

deepDives.title: Audit & rollback
points: Every change writes a row to rule_history with the diff and changed_by.
Rollback is a write of the previous version's body — same propagation path.

bottlenecks.issue: Rule-store outage starves new processes of rules.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Limiter pins last-known-good rules on disk; falls back to those on cold start.
Periodic full reconciliation + version check on every decision.

bottlenecks.issue: A bad rule (limit=0) blocks all traffic instantly.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Validation in control plane + canary rollout to a subset of limiter processes.

talkingPoints: Treat the rule store like a config-management system: changes are reviewable, auditable, and revertable.
Mention that 'shadow' / 'log-only' action lets you test new rules without traffic impact.

flows.name: Operator pushes a new rule
flows.note: Write goes to Rule Store, event bus notifies the fleet, processes pull and hot-swap.
diagram: S->>Rules: GET rule by id, ver

flows.name: Decision with rule cache hit
flows.note: Hot path: the rule is in process; only the counter call touches the network.

steps.title: 5. Edge Enforcement + Async Analytics
steps.description: Move the limiter into the gateway / CDN edge so the decision happens before traffic crosses regions or hits the backend. Decisions are emitted asynchronously to an analytics pipeline for dashboards, alerts, and rule tuning. Multi-region splits global limits (shared counter store, eventually consistent) from per-region limits (local store, fast).
steps.decisionPrompt: Decide what enforcement can run at the gateway or CDN edge and what must remain central, especially for global limits, analytics, and failure mode.

concepts.definition: Make allow/deny decisions at the gateway or CDN edge before traffic reaches backend services.
concepts.whyItMatters: Rejected traffic consumes less regional and backend capacity.

concepts.definition: Choose whether requests are allowed or denied when the limiter dependency is unavailable.
concepts.whyItMatters: The right failure mode depends on whether availability or abuse prevention matters more.

deepDives.title: Edge enforcement
points: Run the limiter as an Envoy filter, nginx module, or CDN edge function (Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge).
Rejected requests don't cross the WAN — saves cost, reduces blast radius of abuse.
Edge processes still talk to the regional counter store; rules are pulled on a slow refresh.

deepDives.title: Multi-region
points: Per-region limit: local counter store, sub-ms.
Global limit: a shared store with async replication; accept ~5% over-allowance as the cost of avoiding cross-region round trips on the hot path.
For strict global limits, route limited principals to a single 'home' region.

deepDives.title: Analytics & feedback loop
points: Every decision (allow or deny) goes to a queue. Sample heavily under sustained DDoS to avoid amplifying load.
Stream processor aggregates per-rule, per-principal, per-region.
Dashboards show top-rejected principals and per-rule pressure; operators use this to tune limits without guessing.

bottlenecks.mitigation: Async emit with bounded in-process buffer; drop oldest on overflow (analytics is best-effort).

bottlenecks.issue: Cross-region counter sync lag allows brief over-allowance.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Explicit SLA on global limits ('within X% over Y minutes'); strict-global routes traffic to one region.

bottlenecks.issue: Limiter itself becomes a target of abuse.
bottlenecks.mitigation: L3/L4 DDoS protection in front; cheap fast-path rejection for blocklisted IPs.

talkingPoints: Mention that the decision stream feeds anomaly detection (sudden deny-rate spikes → adversarial traffic).
Discuss the consistency contract: a freshly-tightened rule may take a few seconds to propagate to all edges.

flows.name: Edge-enforced request — allowed
flows.note: Decision happens at the edge; backend never sees the request count.

flows.name: Edge-enforced request — denied
flows.note: Rejected at the edge with 429; the backend is never contacted, and the decision still emits to analytics.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end rate limiter architecture: enforcement at the gateway or edge, shared low-latency counters, cached versioned rules, a control plane for operator changes, async analytics, dashboards, and clear fail-open/fail-closed behavior.

options.name: Gateway/edge enforcement with shared counters
pros: Rejected traffic is stopped before backend services
Rule changes propagate without redeploying limiters
Counters remain authoritative across limiter instances
Analytics is async and off the hot path
cons: Counter store latency is on the request path
Global limits are harder across regions

options.name: Backend-side limiter library
pros: Simpler to integrate into existing services
Can make endpoint-specific decisions with local context
cons: Unwanted traffic still reaches backend fleet
Every service must adopt the limiter consistently
Harder to protect shared downstream dependencies early
